records.ety: [OE. blowen, AS. blōwan to blossom; akin to OS. blōjan, D. bloeijen, OHG. pluojan, MHG. blüejen, G. blühen, L. florere to flourish, OIr. blath blossom. Cf. Blow to puff, Flourish.]
records.morph: [imp. Blew (blū); p. p. Blown (blōn); p. pr. & vb. n. Blowing.]

defs: To flower; to blossom; to bloom.
quotes: How blows the citron grove.

defs: To cause to blossom; to put forth (blossoms or flowers).
quotes: The odorous banks, that blow Flowers of more mingled hue.

defs: A blossom; a flower; also, a state of blossoming; a mass of blossoms.

records.ety: [OE. blaw, blowe; cf. OHG. bliuwan, pliuwan, to beat, G. bläuen, Goth. bliggwan.]

defs: A forcible stroke with the hand, fist, or some instrument, as a rod, a club, an ax, or a sword.
quotes: Well struck ! there was blow for blow.

defs: A sudden or forcible act or effort; an assault.
quotes: A vigorous blow might win [Hanno's camp].

defs: The infliction of evil; a sudden calamity; something which produces mental, physical, or financial suffering or loss (esp. when sudden); a buffet.
quotes: A most poor man, made tame to fortune's blows.

records.ety: [OE. blawen, blowen, AS. blāwan to blow, as wind; akin to OHG. plājan, G. blähen, to blow up, swell, L. flare to blow, Gr. 'ekflai`nein to spout out, and to E. bladder, blast, inflate, etc., and perh. blow to bloom.]
records.morph: [imp. Blew (blū); p. p. Blown (blōn); p. pr. & vb. n. Blowing.]

defs: To produce a current of air; to move, as air, esp. to move rapidly or with power; as, the wind blows.
quotes: Hark how it rains and blows !

defs: To send forth a forcible current of air, as from the mouth or from a pair of bellows.
To breathe hard or quick; to pant; to puff.
quotes: Here is Mistress Page at the door, sweating and blowing.

defs: To sound on being blown into, as a trumpet.
quotes: There let the pealing organ blow.

defs: To spout water, etc., from the blowholes, as a whale.
To be carried or moved by the wind; as, the dust blows in from the street.
quotes: The grass blows from their graves to thy own.

defs: To talk loudly; to boast; to storm.
quotes: You blow behind my back, but dare not say anything to my face.

defs: To stop functioning due to a failure in an electrical circuit, especially on which breaks the circuit; sometimes used with out; -- used of light bulbs, electronic components, fuses; as, the dome light in the car blew out.
To deflate by sudden loss of air; usually used with out; -- of inflatable tires.
To force a current of air upon with the mouth, or by other means; as, to blow the fire.
To drive by a current air; to impel; as, the tempest blew the ship ashore.
quotes: Off at sea northeast winds blow Sabean odors from the spicy shore.

defs: To cause air to pass through by the action of the mouth, or otherwise; to cause to sound, as a wind instrument; as, to blow a trumpet; to blow an organ; to blow a horn.
quotes: Hath she no husband That will take pains to blow a horn before her?
Boy, blow the pipe until the bubble rise, Then cast it off to float upon the skies.

defs: To clear of contents by forcing air through; as, to blow an egg; to blow one's nose.
To burst, shatter, or destroy by an explosion; -- usually with up, down, open, or similar adverb; as, to blow up a building.
To spread by report; to publish; to disclose; to reveal, intentionally or inadvertently; as, to blow an agent's cover.
quotes: Through the court his courtesy was blown.
His language does his knowledge blow.

defs: To form by inflation; to swell by injecting air; as, to blow bubbles; to blow glass.
To inflate, as with pride; to puff up.
quotes: Look how imagination blows him.

defs: To put out of breath; to cause to blow from fatigue; as, to blow a horse.
To deposit eggs or larvæ upon, or in (meat, etc.).
quotes: To suffer The flesh fly blow my mouth.

defs: To perform an act of fellatio on; to stimulate another's penis with one's mouth; -- usually considered vulgar.
to smoke (e. g. marijuana); to blow pot.
to botch; to bungle; as, he blew his chance at a good job by showing up late for the interview.
to leave; to depart from; as, to blow town.
to squander; as, he blew his inheritance gambling.
quotes: I have blown him up well -- nobody can say I wink at what he does.
How far the very custom of hearing anything spouted withers and blows upon a fine passage, may be seen in those speeches from [Shakespeare's] Henry V. which are current in the mouths of schoolboys.
A lady's maid whose character had been blown upon.

defs: A blowing, esp., a violent blowing of the wind; a gale; as, a heavy blow came on, and the ship put back to port.
The act of forcing air from the mouth, or through or from some instrument; as, to give a hard blow on a whistle or horn; to give the fire a blow with the bellows.
The spouting of a whale.
A single heat or operation of the Bessemer converter.
An egg, or a larva, deposited by a fly on or in flesh, or the act of depositing it.
